items.q: What produces a magnetic field around a wire?
items.correct: A moving electric charge — i.e. an electric current.
items.misconception: The metal in the wire is naturally magnetic.
items.random_wrong: The voltage across the wire creates magnetism.
items.why: Students think magnetism is intrinsic to the wire material rather than a consequence of charge motion.

items.q: What is the threshold frequency in the photoelectric effect?
items.correct: The minimum frequency of light required to eject electrons from a given metal — it equals the work function divided by Planck's constant.
items.misconception: The minimum intensity of light needed to eject electrons.
items.random_wrong: The frequency at which photons travel at the speed of light.
items.why: Students substitute 'intensity' for 'frequency' or describe threshold as a brightness limit, missing the quantum (per-photon) condition.

items.q: Is the cosmological redshift of distant galaxies a true Doppler shift?
items.correct: No — it arises from the expansion of space stretching photon wavelengths during transit, not from the galaxy's motion through space.
items.misconception: Yes — it is the same Doppler shift as a moving siren on Earth.
items.random_wrong: No — it is caused by dust in space absorbing blue light.
items.why: Students learn the Doppler analogy early and don't update to the more accurate metric-expansion picture in cosmology.
It's v ∝ d. “v ∝ 1/d” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: 2.7 K. “0 K” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If H₀ = 70 km/s/Mpc, age of universe (1/H₀) in Gyr (approx):
items.why: The real figure is 14. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
